promote.source: World Health Organization (via World Bank) – with major processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Rapid population growth has outpaced access gains, leaving 42 million more people without clean cooking fuels since 2000

promote.label: People relying on surface water for drinking
promote.desc: Number of people using drinking water directly collected from surface waters.
promote.source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene (2025) – processed by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Nigeria has achieved a massive reduction in unsafe water reliance, cutting surface-water users by over two-thirds since 2000

exclude.reason: Immigrants make up less than 1% of the population across the entire period, making migration a negligible driver of Nigeria's demographic trends.
Captures fixed telephone lines which are obsolete in Nigeria's mobile-first market, showing flat, near-zero adoption
Space launches are a niche activity with negligible numbers for Nigeria across the period

promote.label: Number of internet users
promote.desc: The number of individuals using the Internet.
promote.source: World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database - International Telecommunication Union (ITU), via World Bank (2026); Population based on various sources (2024)
promote.reason: Nigeria has emerged as Africa's largest digital market, with internet users growing to over 77 million

promote.label: Primary-school-age children out of school
promote.desc: Children in the official primary school age range who are not enrolled in either primary or secondary education.
promote.source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics (2025) – with minor processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Nigeria has one of the world's largest populations of out-of-school children, with the absolute number rising over the decade.

exclude.reason: The estimated tuberculosis incidence rate remained completely unchanged at 49 cases per 100,000 people.
HIV prevalence has remained well below 1% of the population throughout the entire period.

promote.label: Universal Health Coverage index
promote.source: World Health Organization - Global Health Observatory (2026) – with minor processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Reflects the steady expansion of healthcare services under Brazil's landmark free universal public health system (SUS).

exclude.reason: The data points for the start and end years are registered as zero, making the trend uninformative.
The data points for the selected years are registered as zero, which does not informatively represent disaster impacts in Brazil.

promote.label: Annual deforestation
promote.source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (2025) – with major processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Deforestation in the Amazon is Brazil's most critical environmental challenge and a focal point of national and global climate policy.

promote.label: Share of workers in informal employment
promote.desc: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries.
promote.source: International Labour Organization – with major processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Informal labor has historically employed around half of Brazil's workforce, though formalization has improved over the last fifteen years.

promote.label: Annual meat production
promote.source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (2025) – with major processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Brazil has emerged as a global agricultural powerhouse, with meat production increasing nearly 14-fold since 1961.

promote.label: Pesticide use per hectare of cropland
promote.source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (2025) – with major processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Brazil's intensive farming model relies on exceptionally high and rising rates of chemical application.

exclude.reason: Terrorism is a negligible phenomenon in Brazil, with virtually zero deaths recorded over the last several decades.
Brazil has not experienced domestic armed conflict deaths during the modern period.

promote.label: Homicides
promote.desc: Number of intentional homicides.
promote.source: UN, World Population Prospects (2024); United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (2025) – with minor processing by Our World in Data
promote.reason: Brazil suffers from an exceptionally high absolute volume of violent crime, accounting for over 40,000 homicides annually.

exclude.reason: The share of foreign-born residents in Brazil is extremely low (under 1%) and has remained virtually unchanged for decades.
Extremely low absolute numbers of annual space launches (1–2 objects) make this metric uninformative for Brazil.
